target.title: fix(browser): move ssrf-runtime-internal exports to public ssrf-runti…
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves
closes #96639
Fixes the browser plugin lazy-load crash where `openclaw browser status` and any browser agent tool fail with:

```
Package subpath './plugin-sdk/ssrf-runtime-internal' is not defined by "exports"
in openclaw/package.json imported from openclaw\dist\cdp.helpers-B5T7VOSu.js
```

## Why This Change Was Made

`registerManagedProxyBrowserCdpBypass` and `fetchConfiguredLocalOriginWithSsrFGuard` were only exported from the private `openclaw/plugin-sdk/ssrf-runtime-internal` subpath. Export both functions from the public `openclaw/plugin-sdk/ssrf-runtime` path and update consumer imports.

## What Changed

9 files, +10/−15 lines. No `package.json` or config changes.

## Evidence

Environment: Linux x64, Node v22.19.0, Chromium 148.

### Before (origin/main) — confirmed broken

```text
# dist file excluded from package
$ ls dist/plugin-sdk/ssrf-runtime-internal.js
FILE NOT FOUND

# Node.js exports map rejects the import
$ node -e "import('openclaw/plugin-sdk/ssrf-runtime-internal')"
Error: Package subpath './plugin-sdk/ssrf-runtime-internal' is not defined by "exports"

# Two functions NOT in public ssrf-runtime
$ node --import tsx -e "
const mod = await import('openclaw/plugin-sdk/ssrf-runtime');
console.log('registerManagedProxyBrowserCdpBypass:', typeof mod.registerManagedProxyBrowserCdpBypass);
console.log('fetchConfiguredLocalOriginWithSsrFGuard:', typeof mod.fetchConfiguredLocalOriginWithSsrFGuard);
"
registerManagedProxyBrowserCdpBypass: undefined
fetchConfiguredLocalOriginWithSsrFGuard: undefined
```

### After (fix/browser-ssrf-runtime-import) — confirmed fixed

```text
# Both functions importable from public ssrf-runtime
$ node --import tsx -e "
const mod = await import('openclaw/plugin-sdk/ssrf-runtime');
console.log('registerManagedProxyBrowserCdpBypass:', typeof mod.registerManagedProxyBrowserCdpBypass);
console.log('fetchConfiguredLocalOriginWithSsrFGuard:', typeof mod.fetchConfiguredLocalOriginWithSsrFGuard);
"
registerManagedProxyBrowserCdpBypass: function
fetchConfiguredLocalOriginWithSsrFGuard: function

# E2E: browser plugin lazy-loads, gateway returns full status
$ pnpm build && pnpm openclaw gateway start && pnpm openclaw browser status
profile:          openclaw
enabled:          true
transport:        cdp
cdpUrl:           http://127.0.0.1:18800
detectedBrowser:  chrome
detectedPath:     /usr/bin/google-chrome-stable
EXIT: 0

# Private subpath infrastructure unchanged
$ grep "!dist/plugin-sdk/ssrf-runtime-internal" package.json
"!dist/plugin-sdk/ssrf-runtime-internal.js"
```

### Unit tests

| Test | Result |
|------|--------|
| ollama embedding-provider (25) | 25/25 passed |
| browser cdp-proxy-bypass (28) | 27/28 (1 pre-existing env issue) |
| contract guardrail (21) | 21/21 passed |
| surface-report --check | exit 0 |

### Test conclusion

| Check | Before (origin/main) | After (fix) |
|-------|---------------------|-------------|
| Public import of both functions | `undefined` | `function` |
| `openclaw browser status` | — | exit 0, full output |
| Unit tests | — | 73/74 passed |
| Private dist exclusion | unchanged | unchanged |

## Root Cause

`registerManagedProxyBrowserCdpBypass` (`a37ebb2d49`) and `fetchConfiguredLocalOriginWithSsrFGuard` (`fd2a9adbe6`) were placed in the private `ssrf-runtime-internal` module. Its dist artifact was excluded via `!dist/` (added by `2229122077`) and it had no `package.json` exports entry. Node.js native module resolution rejected the import at runtime.

target.title: fix(agents): accept multi-line ANNOUNCE_SKIP on final line
target.body:
## Summary

- Fix `isAnnounceSkip()` to also accept multi-line sub-agent output where `ANNOUNCE_SKIP` appears as the final line after a newline, not just as the sole content.
- Add focused unit tests for `sessions-send-tokens.ts` covering exact, whitespace-padded, multi-line, and negative cases.
- Add an e2e regression test for the multi-line announce-skip scenario.

Closes #74071

## Problem

Sub-agents that produce a DM summary block followed by `ANNOUNCE_SKIP` on the final line had their entire summary leak into the parent session as an announcement. The strict equality check `(text ?? "").trim() === ANNOUNCE_SKIP_TOKEN` only matched when the model emitted *just* the token, but real sub-agent outputs typically end with the token after a summary block.

## Fix

`isAnnounceSkip()` now checks two conditions:
1. **Exact match** (existing): trimmed text equals the token
2. **Final-line match** (new): trimmed text ends with the token on its own line (after the last `\n`), with the final line also trimmed to handle whitespace

This preserves all existing behavior (exact and whitespace-padded) and adds the realistic multi-line case reported in the issue. The reporter had been running this patch locally since 2026-04-14 with no regressions.

## Test plan

- `pnpm test src/agents/tools/sessions-send-tokens.test.ts` — 16 new focused unit tests (all pass)
- `pnpm test src/agents/subagent-announce.test.ts` — 9 existing tests (all pass)
- `pnpm test src/agents/subagent-announce-output.test.ts` — 3 existing tests (all pass)
- `pnpm test src/agents/openclaw-tools.sessions.test.ts` — 15 existing tests (all pass)

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: sub-agent output ending with a final-line `ANNOUNCE_SKIP` should suppress completion announcement delivery, while `ANNOUNCE_SKIP` followed by more text should not be treated as a silent sentinel.
Real environment tested: local OpenClaw source checkout using fork PR head `6440ea6b4f`, plus reporter production confirmation on issue #74071's canonical sub-agent workflow.
Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```text
node --import tsx -e '<script imports isAnnounceSkip, checks single-line sentinel, multiline text ending with ANNOUNCE_SKIP, and ANNOUNCE_SKIP followed by more text>'
```

Evidence after fix:

```json
{
  "branch": "fork/fix/announce-skip-multiline-74071",
  "head": "6440ea6b4f",
  "command": "node --import tsx isAnnounceSkip proof",
  "cases": [
    {
      "name": "single-line sentinel",
      "lineCount": 1,
      "result": true
    },
    {
      "name": "multiline final-line sentinel",
      "lineCount": 3,
      "result": true
    },
    {
      "name": "sentinel not final",
      "lineCount": 2,
      "result": false
    }
  ]
}
```

Observed result after fix: a final-line `ANNOUNCE_SKIP` suppresses announcement, including after a summary block; a non-final sentinel does not suppress announcement. The reporter also confirmed an equivalent local patch has run for about 5 weeks across about 5,000+ sub-agent completions with zero observed regressions.
What was not tested: This proof exercises the production sentinel parser locally rather than sending a live sub-agent completion through an external channel.

## Notes

- `isReplySkip()` has the same strict-equality pattern but is not changed here since there is no reported issue for it in the A2A ping-pong flow. It could receive the same treatment in a follow-up if needed.
- Docs currently describe exact-token suppression (`docs/tools/subagents.md`); consider aligning docs if final-line token support becomes the intended contract.

target.title: feat(cron): configurable minimum interval guardrail for recurring jobs
target.body:
## Summary

Adds an opt-in `cron.minInterval` setting that enforces a **minimum interval between fires for recurring cron jobs**. 
For hosted OpenClaw, we want to define guardrails for the users, and very frequent cron is wasteful/costly. So we want it to fail fast.
With two layers driven by the one config value:

1. **Creation-time rejection (UX layer):** creating or editing an `every`/`cron` job whose schedule falls below the floor fails with a clear error, so the agent or CLI caller immediately picks a saner cadence. For `cron` expressions this checks the smallest gap across sampled upcoming fires — documented as best-effort feedback, since bounded sampling cannot prove an expression's true minimum gap.
2. **Fire-time floor (enforcement layer):** the scheduler paces every re-arm so consecutive fires of a recurring job stay at least `minInterval` apart (start-to-start, launchd `ThrottleInterval`-style). This is the contract the docs promise: it covers jobs created before the limit was configured, cron expressions whose tight gaps escape sampling, restarts, and update re-arms. Deferred fires log a warning naming the job.

Motivation: hosted/operator deployments need a real resource limit on cron frequency, not just a creation-side suggestion — while still blocking bad schedules up front so users learn the limit at the moment they can act on it. Mirrors the `cron.maxConcurrentRuns` operator-guardrail pattern.

## What changed

- **Config surface**: `cron.minInterval` (`CronConfig` type, zod schema + duration validation, help text, label, docs). Duration string (`30s`, `5m`, `1h`) or milliseconds; default `0`/unset = no limit.
- **Resolver**: `resolveCronMinIntervalMs()` in `src/config/cron-limits.ts`.
- **Creation validation**: `assertScheduleMeetsMinInterval()` in the cron service, from `createJob` and schedule-changing `update`s. Maps to `INVALID_REQUEST` in the gateway handler; the cron tool description tells agents to increase the interval instead of retrying.
- **Fire-time floor**: `minIntervalFloorAtMs()` (`src/cron/service/jobs.ts`) applied at every `nextRunAtMs` writer:
  - completion + error-backoff re-arm (`applyJobResult`, timer.ts) with a deferral warning;
  - `update()` re-arm (ops.ts) — a bare `{enabled: true}` update can no longer reset a deferred fire;
  - maintenance/repair recompute (`recomputeJobNextRunAtMs`) so `repairFutureCronNextRunAtMs` cannot undo deferrals (floored values also skip repair, avoiding per-tick rework);
  - restart paths: startup missed-slot catch-up and pending-backoff deferral respect the floor.
- **Scope decisions**: one-shot `at` jobs exempt; manual/forced runs (`preserveSchedule`) never shift the schedule and are not floored; transient-failure retries follow `cron.retry`; a 2s dispatch slack keeps schedules whose cadence exactly equals the floor on their natural slots (no drift, no warning spam); floor `0`/unset preserves shipped behavior exactly.

## Config example

```json5
{
  cron: {
    minInterval: "5m", // recurring jobs neither validate nor fire more often than every 5 minutes
  },
}
```

Creation error shape: `cron schedule fires every 30s, below the minimum interval of 5m`.

## Verification

Run locally (clean):

- `oxfmt --check` + `oxlint` on touched files — clean
- `tsgo -p tsconfig.core.json` and `-p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json` — clean for touched code (two pre-existing errors in `src/config/io.ts`/`src/secrets/config-io.ts` come from the branch base and are already fixed on `main`; rebase resolves)
- Full cron subsystem: `src/cron` — 101 test files, 1002 tests, all green
- New behavior tests `src/cron/service.min-interval-floor.test.ts`: pre-existing every/cron jobs paced + warned; error-path pacing; no-floor regression guard; at-floor schedule keeps natural cadence under dispatch jitter; force runs preserve the schedule; enable-toggle update keeps the floor; startup catch-up does not replay a missed slot inside the floor window
- Existing suites re-run green: `jobs.min-interval`, `service.jobs`, `jobs.apply-patch`, `ops`, `ops.regression`, `timer`, `timer.regression`, `armtimer-tight-loop`, `every-jobs-fire`, issue regressions 13992/17852/66019, `cron-limits`, `zod-schema.cron-min-interval`, `schema.help.quality`

Multi-agent adversarial review of the floor change ran pre-push; confirmed findings (force-run schedule shift, enable-toggle bypass, restart catch-up/backoff bypasses, at-floor jitter drift, maintenance repair undoing deferrals) are all fixed and regression-tested above.

target.title: fix(media): allow private openai compatible audio transcription endpoints
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Audio media-understanding transcription could target self-hosted OpenAI-compatible endpoints, but audio config did not expose the private-network request opt-in used by guarded provider HTTP.
- Why it matters: Trusted local, RFC1918, or tailnet Whisper-style endpoints need explicit private-network access without globally relaxing media request SSRF policy.
- What changed: Added audio-only request config support for `tools.media.audio.request.allowPrivateNetwork`, aligned schema/generated metadata/docs, threaded audio request overrides into provider execution, and aligned OpenAI-compatible loopback audio auth with the chat-completion local auth pattern by using the shared `custom-local` marker only for loopback OpenAI-compatible audio bases.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): Image and video media request config still reject `allowPrivateNetwork`, private-network access remains disabled by default, explicit `request.auth` still injects the configured auth header, and media/audio does not add the Ollama-specific local-auth branch.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [x] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes N/A
- Related N/A
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Audio media config reused the generic media request schema/type path, which rejected `allowPrivateNetwork`, while provider execution needed an audio-specific trusted request override path. The local no-auth path also needed to follow the existing chat-completion pattern: synthesize the shared `custom-local` marker for loopback OpenAI-compatible endpoints, then avoid sending provider-default auth for that marker.
- Missing detection / guardrail: Existing schema and runner tests did not cover audio-specific private-network request overrides or loopback OpenAI-compatible audio endpoints without provider auth.
- Contributing context (if known): The config docs baseline and audio field metadata are generated/aligned surfaces for this config contract.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/config/schema.test.ts`, `src/media-understanding/openai-compatible-audio.test.ts`, `src/media-understanding/runner.proxy.test.ts`, `src/media-understanding/shared.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: Audio config accepts and forwards `allowPrivateNetwork`, image/video reject it, per-model audio request config works, loopback OpenAI-compatible audio can synthesize `custom-local` when provider auth is absent, synthetic local audio auth sends no provider-default `Authorization`, explicit request auth still wins, and Ollama does not get the media/audio local-auth branch.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: These tests exercise the config contract and provider request assembly without needing a live transcription service.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Users can opt audio transcription requests into private-network endpoints with `tools.media.audio.request.allowPrivateNetwork` and per-model audio request config. Loopback OpenAI-compatible audio endpoints such as `http://127.0.0.1:8000/v1` can run without provider auth, matching local OpenAI-compatible chat-completion providers. The OpenAI provider docs now show the loopback example base URL.

## Diagram (if applicable)

N/A

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: The new network capability is an explicit audio-only opt-in for trusted private endpoints; defaults remain blocked by the SSRF guard and image/video still reject the private-network flag. Local no-auth audio is restricted to loopback OpenAI-compatible audio providers using the existing non-secret `custom-local` marker. Explicit request auth still injects the configured header when present.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux 6.1.0-44-cloud-amd64 x86_64
- Runtime/container: Node v22.22.2, pnpm 10.33.0
- Model/provider: OpenAI-compatible audio transcription config path
- Integration/channel (if any): Media understanding audio attachments
- Relevant config (redacted): `tools.media.audio.request.allowPrivateNetwork`, `tools.media.audio.request.auth`, `tools.media.audio.models[].baseUrl`

### Steps

1. Parse audio, image, and video media config shapes through the runtime zod schema.
2. Run audio provider execution with provider-level, audio-level, and per-model request overrides.
3. Run provider HTTP request assembly with synthetic local auth and explicit request auth.

### Expected

- Audio request config accepts and forwards private-network opt-in only when explicitly configured.
- Image/video reject `allowPrivateNetwork` under media request config.
- Loopback OpenAI-compatible audio can use the shared synthetic local auth marker when provider auth is absent.
- Synthetic local auth does not become a provider-default `Authorization` header.
- Explicit request auth overrides synthetic local auth and injects the configured header.
- Ollama does not receive a media/audio local-auth special case.

### Actual

- Matches expected in local tests and changed-gate validation.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Local validation:

- `pnpm docs:list`
- `pnpm test src/media-understanding/openai-compatible-audio.test.ts src/media-understanding/runner.proxy.test.ts src/media-understanding/shared.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/agents/model-auth.ts src/media-understanding/runner.entries.ts src/media-understanding/runner.proxy.test.ts src/media-understanding/runner.test-mocks.ts docs/providers/openai.md docs/nodes/audio.md CHANGELOG.md src/media-understanding/openai-compatible-audio.ts src/media-understanding/openai-compatible-audio.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- `pnpm check:changed`

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

Set up a (WhisperLive)[https://github.com/collabora/WhisperLive] server locally. Modified openclaw.json to point to it with `media.audio.request.allowPrivateNetwork = true`:

```
"media": {
      "audio": {
        "enabled": true,
        "language": "en",
        "models": [
          {
            "provider": "openai",
            "model": "whisper-1",
            "baseUrl": "http://127.0.0.1:8000/v1",
          }
        ],
        "request": {
          "allowPrivateNetwork": true,
        }
      }
```

Verified audio transcription:

```
$ openclaw capability audio transcribe --file tmp/media-test/transcription-test.wav --language en --json
{
  "ok": true,
  "capability": "audio.transcribe",
  "transport": "local",
  "attempts": [],
  "outputs": [
    {
      "path": "/home/openclaw/.openclaw/workspace/tmp/media-test/transcription-test.wav",
      "text": "Open Claw Media Transcription Test. The passphrase is Brass Lobster 7.",
      "kind": "audio.transcription"
    }
  ]
}
```



## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

No PR review conversations currently exist.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) Yes, optional new audio request config only
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Users could point audio transcription at private endpoints they do not fully trust.
  - Mitigation: The private-network exception is explicit, audio-only, disabled by default, documented as trusted-endpoint-only, and covered by schema/runtime tests.
- Risk: Synthetic local auth could accidentally be sent as provider-default auth.
  - Mitigation: OpenAI-compatible audio now uses the shared chat-completion local auth marker and omits provider-default auth for that marker while preserving explicit request auth overrides.

target.title: feat(openrouter): inject cache_control for closed-source qwen models
target.body:
## Summary

Extend the existing OpenRouter cache_control wrapper to cover Alibaba's closed-source qwen commercial line (`qwen3-max` / `qwen3-plus` / `qwen3-flash` and future versioned tiers), in addition to `anthropic/*` models.

## Why this is safe

Closed-source qwen models on OpenRouter are served **exclusively by Alibaba (DashScope)**, which implements Anthropic-compatible `cache_control: { type: "ephemeral" }` markers. Routing is therefore deterministic — the marker is guaranteed to take effect, so we can inject it unconditionally with no need for explicit `provider.only` routing.

Open-source qwen models are excluded because OpenRouter routes them across multiple upstreams (Together, DeepInfra, Fireworks, etc.), most of which do not support `cache_control`. Models with middle qualifiers like `qwen3-coder-plus` and the unversioned rolling aliases (`qwen-max`, `qwen-plus`, `qwen-turbo`, `qwen-flash`) are also excluded for now because their cache_control support is less predictable across snapshots.

## Match rule

```
/^qwen\/qwen\d+(\.\d+){0,2}-(plus|max|flash)(-preview|-\d{4}(-\d{2}-\d{2})?)?$/
```

Covers:
- `qwen/qwen3-max`, `qwen/qwen3-plus`, `qwen/qwen3-flash`
- Future minor/patch versions (`qwen3.5-max`, `qwen4-max`, ...)
- Dated snapshots (`qwen3-max-2025-09-23`, `qwen3-max-2507`)
- Preview tags (`qwen3.6-max-preview`)

Excludes (covered by tests):
- Open-source: `qwen3-235b-a22b`, `qwen3-coder`, `qwq-32b`, `qwen-2.5-72b-instruct`
- Middle qualifiers: `qwen3-coder-plus`, `qwen3-vl-max`
- Unversioned aliases: `qwen-max`, `qwen-plus`, `qwen-turbo`, `qwen-flash`
- Turbo tier: `qwen3-turbo`

## Test plan

- [x] Unit tests cover the truth table (7 cases, all passing)
- [x] Existing `proxy-stream-wrappers.test.ts` still passes
- [x] Live E2E verification — see results below

## Live E2E results

Tested against real OpenRouter + Alibaba upstream with `openrouter/qwen/qwen3.6-plus`, captured via `mitmdump`:

**Request body** (after wrapper patch — confirmed in captured TLS payload):
```json
{
  "model": "qwen/qwen3.6-plus",
  "messages": [
    {
      "role": "system",
      "content": [{
        "type": "text",
        "text": "...39228 chars...",
        "cache_control": { "type": "ephemeral" }
      }]
    },
    { "role": "user", "content": "Say hi in one word" }
  ]
}
```

**Response usage** (two identical-prefix calls, ~5s apart):

| Call | prompt_tokens | cached_tokens | cache_write_tokens | cost |
|------|---------------|---------------|---------------------|------|
| 1    | 8030          | 0             | **8014** ← write    | $0.003795 |
| 2    | 8030          | **8014** ← hit | 0                  | $0.000827 |

Cache hit on second call → **78% cost reduction** on the cached prefix portion. Confirms:

1. Wrapper injects `cache_control: { type: "ephemeral" }` on the system message for closed-source qwen
2. OpenRouter passes the marker through to Alibaba upstream
3. Alibaba honors the marker, writes the cache, and serves identical-prefix requests from cache

target.title: fix(tui): deduplicate assistant messages across sessions.changed reload
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

When a Telegram (or other external channel) run completes, the gateway sends a `sessions.changed` (reason: "new") event. The TUI responds by calling `clearTrackedRunState()` + `loadHistory()`, which does `chatLog.clearAll()` then replays assistant rows via `chatLog.finalizeAssistant(text)` — **without a runId**. A late `chat.final` for the same run arrives afterward with a runId, and since `chatLog.finalizeAssistant(text, runId)` cannot match the no-runId replayed row in `streamingRuns`, it appends a new `AssistantMessageComponent` — producing a visible duplicate assistant reply (#96967).

The fix operates at the **event handler level only**, using **runId-based surrender tracking** — late events for surrendered runIds are suppressed by exact runId match rather than by ChatLog text heuristics. This absorbs the precise approach from closed sibling PRs #96979 and #96986.

Fixes #96967

## Why This Change Was Made

**v1 (original #96980)** used a ChatLog text-matching heuristic: `lastHistoryAssistant` tracked history replay rows, and a same-text live final within a 30s TTL was suppressed. ClawSweeper flagged this as a **P1 false-suppression risk** — it could theoretically hide a legitimate same-text final from a *different* run.

**v2 (this commit)** drops the ChatLog text layer entirely:

| Event | v1 (text heuristic) | v2 (runId surrender) |
|---|---|---|
| sessions.changed "new" fires | ChatLog marker set on history replay | sessionRuns surrendered into Set |
| Late delta for surrendered run | Text match + adjacency guards | **Suppress by runId** |
| Late final for surrendered run | Text match + adjacency + TTL | **Suppress render + mark finalized** |
| loadHistory restored as streaming | Not detectable | `hasStreamingRun()` → **pass through** |
| Different-run same-text final | **P1 risk: suppressed** | **Not surrendered → passes** |

**Compared to sibling implementations:**

| | #96986 (closed) | #96979 (closed) | This (v2) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Layer | ChatLog only | Event handler only | Event handler only |
| Method | Text match + 5s TTL | runId surrender + 15s timer | runId surrender (no timer) |
| `hasStreamingRun()` | No | Yes | Yes |
| Surrender scope | N/A | sessionRuns+finalized+active+pending | sessionRuns only |
| `chatFinalizedRuns` persistence | No | No | Yes (retained from v1) |

Key design decisions:
- **No TTL timer**: Entries are removed on first matching event (typically <1s after surrender). No stale-marker risk.
- **Surrender scope: sessionRuns only**: `chatFinalizedRuns` / `chatFinalizedRunsWithDisplay` already handle finalized runs correctly.
- **ChatLog API minimal**: Only `hasStreamingRun()` added; old marker logic fully removed (net −55 in ChatLog).

## User Impact

- TUI users on external channels (Telegram, Discord, etc.) who previously saw duplicate assistant replies after `sessions.changed` reloads will see a single reply.
- No configuration changes, no new flags or behavioral knobs.
- ChatLog has a new `hasStreamingRun()` method for the event handler contract.

## Summary

- Drop `lastHistoryAssistant` / `HISTORY_REPLAY_DEDUPE_MS` from ChatLog — net **−55 lines**
- Add `ChatLog.hasStreamingRun()` — **+5 lines**
- Add `surrenderedToHistoryRunIds` Set + surrender in `handleSessionsChangedEvent`
- Add surrender check in `handleChatEvent`: suppress by exact runId match for surrendered non-streaming runs
- Retain `chatFinalizedRuns` / `chatFinalizedRunsWithDisplay` from v1 for post-reload delta/error dedup
- 6 new surrender-tracking tests + 6 `hasStreamingRun` tests; drop 7 ChatLog text-dedupe tests
- Changed: 4 files (vs `main`: `chat-log.ts` +5, `chat-log.test.ts` +45, `tui-event-handlers.ts` +80/−3, `tui-event-handlers.test.ts` +266)

## Evidence

- `pnpm test src/tui/components/chat-log.test.ts` — **26 passed** (6 hasStreamingRun)
- `pnpm test src/tui/tui-event-handlers.test.ts` — **86 passed** (6 surrender tracking + 3 sessions.changed regression)
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.tui-pty.config.ts` — **13 passed**
- `pnpm build` — OK
- Surrender probe logs (unit test, reverted before commit): **5/5 branches verified**

### Real behavior proof

Real environment tested: Linux 4.19.112-2.el8, Node 24.15.0, branch `fix/issue-96967-tui-duplicate-assistant`, commit `0c334cc8a9`

#### External-channel simulation — single reply proof

An external run was triggered via `openclaw agent` (which calls Gateway RPC `chat.send` — the same mechanism Telegram/Discord channel plugins use). The TUI was subscribed to the same session as a passive observer.

```bash
pnpm openclaw gateway --port 18789 &      # production config, deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro
pnpm openclaw tui --session main          # TUI connected (local discovery)

# In a separate shell: external run
OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_TOKEN=<token> pnpm openclaw agent \
  --session-key "agent:main_assistant:main" \
  --message "探针测试" --json
```

**TUI terminal output** (captured via `script`, ANSI cleaned):

```
openclaw tui - ws://127.0.0.1:18789 - agent main_assistant (芙莉莲) - session main
session agent:main_assistant:main
gateway connected | idle
agent main_assistant (芙莉莲) | session main | deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro

 ⠋ running • 0s | connected
收到                                                    ← agent message arrives
 ⠹ streaming • 0s | connected
 ⠼ streaming • 0s | connected
 ⠼ finishing context • 0s | connected
 ⠴ finishing context • 0s | connected
 ⠦ finishing context • 0s | connected
 ⠧ finishing context • 0s | connected
 connected | idle                                       ← exactly ONE reply, no duplicate
```

#### Surrender tracking probe proof (temporary probes, reverted before commit)

Temporary `fs.appendFileSync` probes inserted at key handler entry points in `tui-event-handlers.ts`. **Reverted before commit** (`git diff` confirms clean).

**Captured from unit test execution (real `createEventHandlers` code path):**

```
SURRENDER reason=new sessionRuns=[run-active] totalSurrendered=1
MATCH surrendered runId=run-active state=delta hasStreamingRun=false
SUPPRESS delta runId=run-active

SURRENDER reason=new sessionRuns=[run-active] totalSurrendered=1
MATCH surrendered runId=run-active state=delta hasStreamingRun=true → PASS (streaming restored)

SURRENDER reason=new sessionRuns=[run-active] totalSurrendered=1
MATCH surrendered runId=run-active state=final hasStreamingRun=false
SUPPRESS final runId=run-active

SURRENDER reason=new sessionRuns=[run-active] totalSurrendered=1
MATCH surrendered runId=run-active state=aborted hasStreamingRun=false
SUPPRESS aborted runId=run-active

SURRENDER reason=new sessionRuns=[] totalSurrendered=0  — empty (already finalized, correct)
```

#### v1 Gateway event-order proof (from original #96980, still valid)

```
2026-06-26T16:01:24.890Z sessions.changed reason=send  → skipped
2026-06-26T16:01:34.855Z sessions.changed phase=start → skipped (no reason field)
2026-06-26T16:01:37.839Z chat.final runId=183f252f... textLen=29
2026-06-26T16:01:38.102Z sessions.changed phase=end   → skipped (no reason field)
```

**Event ordering confirmed**: sessions.changed(send) → sessions.changed(start) → **chat.final** → sessions.changed(end). The `chat.final` arrives between lifecycle start/end — matching the external-channel race pattern from #96967.

### Observed result after fix

1. **One visible reply** — TUI shows `connected | idle` after a single assistant reply, no duplicate
2. **Surrender tracking active** — 5/5 probe branches verified: delta suppress, final suppress, streaming-restored pass, aborted suppress, empty-surrender
3. **No TTL dependency** — entries removed on first matching event (no stale-marker edge case)
4. **All 125 tests pass**: 26 ChatLog + 86 event-handler + 13 PTY

### Coverage of the #96967 race

| Layer | Evidence |
|-------|----------|
| TUI terminal (real Gateway) | External `openclaw agent` → Gateway → TUI — single reply confirmed |
| Surrender tracking probes | Unit test execution — SURRENDER→MATCH→SUPPRESS chain (5/5 branches) |
| ChatLog `hasStreamingRun` | 6 unit tests — restored/finalized/dropped/cleared/default/clearAll states |
| Event-handler state machine | 86 unit tests — surrender check + sessions.changed regression |
| Real `runTui()` loop | 13 PTY fake-backend tests — `/reset` triggers `sessions.changed` reload |

What was not tested: Telegram channel E2E roundtrip (requires Telegram bot backend and live channel setup).

target.title: fix(sandbox): skip read-only skill mounts when user binds target the same container path
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `openclaw.json` `sandbox.docker.binds` entries targeting reserved container paths (e.g. `/workspace/.agents/skills`) cause the Docker daemon to reject container creation with `Duplicate mount point`. The internal `resolveReadOnlyWorkspaceSkillMounts` unconditionally appends read-only skill mounts without checking whether a user-defined bind already targets the same container path.
- Why now: Any user with custom docker binds that happen to overlap a skill mount path cannot launch sandbox containers at all. The gateway fails before the agent can start.
- What changed:
  - New `filterReadOnlyWorkspaceSkillMountsByBinds()` in `workspace-mounts.ts` parses user bind specs and excludes skill mounts whose container path is already targeted
  - `ensureSandboxContainer` applies the filter before config hash computation and container creation
  - `ensureSandboxBrowser` applies the filter before browser container creation
  - `buildSandboxFsMounts` applies the filter to keep the FS path resolution table consistent
- What did NOT change: Guard mounts in `agent-tools.ts` are unchanged — they don't generate Docker `-v` args. The `appendWorkspaceMountArgs` fallback path is untouched because production callers always pass `includeReadOnlyWorkspaceSkillMounts: false`. No new dependencies added.
- Outcome: User binds that conflict with skill mount paths no longer cause `Duplicate mount point`. User binds take precedence. Config hashes are computed from the effective mount list.
- Reviewer focus: `workspace-mounts.ts` `filterReadOnlyWorkspaceSkillMountsByBinds` function, and the three integration points in `docker.ts`, `browser.ts`, `fs-paths.ts`.

## Linked context

Closes #93854

Was this requested by a maintainer?
- No, but the issue has clear reproduction steps and root cause analysis.

## Root Cause

- Root cause: `resolveReadOnlyWorkspaceSkillMounts` unconditionally appends internal read-only skill mounts for `rw` workspaces. When a user bind targets the same container path, `appendReadOnlyWorkspaceSkillMountArgs` adds a second `-v` spec for the same Docker target, and the daemon rejects the container.
- Missing detection / guardrail: No pre-existing check for container-path overlap between user binds and internal skill mounts. The project's comment-based mount ordering tests assumed Docker would accept duplicate targets and "last one wins", but Docker rejects duplicate mount points.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: `openclaw.json` `sandbox.docker.binds` with `/host/custom-skills:/workspace/skills:rw` must not produce a second `-v ...:/workspace/skills:ro` — that second mount would cause Docker to reject the container with `Duplicate mount point`.
- Real environment tested: Linux x64, Node 22.19.0, pnpm 11.2.2, Docker 20.10.0, branch `fix/sandbox-bind-conflict`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  ```bash
  node --import tsx scripts/e2e-sandbox-bind-conflict.mjs
  pnpm test src/agents/sandbox/
  ```
- Evidence after fix (real Docker container create + docker inspect):
  ```text
  $ node --import tsx scripts/e2e-sandbox-bind-conflict.mjs
  Workspace: /tmp/openclaw-e2e-IGDwq2

  --- Skill mounts ---
  Raw: [ '/tmp/openclaw-e2e-IGDwq2/skills -> /workspace/skills' ]
  Filtered: (none — conflicting mount filtered out)

  --- Docker args ---
    create
    --name
    oc-e2e-bind-1782723336265
    --label
    openclaw.e2e=1
    --workdir
    /workspace
    -v /tmp/openclaw-e2e-IGDwq2:/workspace
    -v /tmp/openclaw-e2e-IGDwq2/custom-mount:/workspace/skills:rw
    e2e-sleep:latest
    infinity

  --- Duplicate check ---
  ✅ No duplicate container paths in -v args

  --- docker create oc-e2e-bind-1782723336265 ---
  ✅ Container created
  Mount destinations: /workspace /workspace/skills
  /workspace/skills count: 1 ✅
  Mount source for /workspace/skills: /tmp/openclaw-e2e-IGDwq2/custom-mount
  Source is custom bind host: ✅
  ```

  ```text
  $ pnpm test src/agents/sandbox/
   Test Files  22 passed (22)
        Tests  224 passed (224)
     Start at  17:40:41
     Duration  9.90s
  ```
- Observed result after fix: The conflicting `/workspace/skills` skill mount is absent from the filtered output. Docker `create` succeeds — no `Duplicate mount point` error. `docker inspect` confirms `/workspace/skills` appears exactly once with the user's custom bind host as the source. All 224 sandbox unit/integration tests pass.
- What was not tested: Live end-to-end with the full OpenClaw gateway and a real agent session. The proof exercises the exact production code path for mount resolution + actual Docker container creation.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?
- `node --import tsx scripts/e2e-sandbox-bind-conflict.mjs` (real Docker container create + inspect)
- `pnpm test src/agents/sandbox/` (224 tests, 22 files)
- `pnpm test src/agents/sandbox/workspace-mounts.test.ts`
- `pnpm test src/agents/sandbox/fs-paths.test.ts`
- `pnpm test src/agents/agent-tools.sandbox-mounted-paths.workspace-only.test.ts`

What regression coverage was added or updated?
- Added 8 new unit tests for `filterReadOnlyWorkspaceSkillMountsByBinds`:
  1. `returns all mounts when binds is undefined`
  2. `returns all mounts when binds is empty`
  3. `filters out a mount whose container path matches a user bind`
  4. `filters out multiple mounts when multiple binds conflict`
  5. `returns all mounts when no bind targets conflict`
  6. `filters out all mounts when every container path is targeted by a bind`
  7. `handles rw binds (no ro option) correctly`
  8. `normalizes container paths before comparing`
- Updated 2 existing integration tests:
  1. `docker.config-hash-recreate.test.ts`: asserts conflicting mount is absent instead of appended last
  2. `browser.create.test.ts`: same update for the browser sandbox path
- Added 1 live proof script: `scripts/demo-sandbox-bind-filter.mjs`

What failed before this fix, if known?
- Docker daemon returned `Duplicate mount point` error when a user bind targeted the same container path as an internal skill mount. Container creation failed entirely — the agent could not start.

target.title: Fix/redact app password overmasking 94254
target.body:
Fixes #94254

## Summary

`STRUCTURED_APP_PASSWORD_FIELD_RE` in `src/logging/redact.ts` was too broad: it included generic field names (`text`, `content`, `message`, `error`, `errorMessage`, `detail`, `details`, `reason`) that triggered Apple app-specific password detection on ordinary text fields. Any kebab-case identifier with four 4-letter segments (e.g. `help-desk-team-page`) in these fields was silently masked, corrupting persisted transcript content.

### Fix

Remove the generic field names from the regex, keeping only Apple-specific fields. Apple passwords in credential fields are still protected by `STRUCTURED_SECRET_FIELD_RE`.

### Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `src/logging/redact.ts` | Remove `text|content|message|error|errorMessage|detail|details|reason` from regex |
| `src/logging/redact.test.ts` | Add 2 tests + update 1 existing test expectation |

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: Ordinary kebab-case identifiers like `help-desk-team-page` and `kube-node-pool-spec` silently masked in `text`/`content`/`message` fields, corrupting persisted transcript content

**Real environment tested**: local source checkout, Node.js v24.13.1, Linux x64

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**: `node --input-type=module` calling `redactSecrets()` directly (see terminal output below)

```
node --input-type=module <<'EOF'
import { redactSecrets } from "./src/logging/redact.js";
const r1 = redactSecrets({ text: "open the help-desk-team-page link" });
console.log("generic field:", r1.text);
const r2 = redactSecrets({ apple: "password is abcd-efgh-ijkl-mnop here" });
console.log("apple field masked:", !r2.apple.includes("abcd-efgh-ijkl-mnop"));
EOF
```

**Evidence after fix**: Terminal output confirms kebab-case identifiers in generic text fields are preserved, while Apple password patterns in Apple-specific fields remain masked.

**Observed result after fix**:

| Input | Field | Result |
|-------|-------|--------|
| `"open the help-desk-team-page link"` | `text` | preserved |
| `"kube-node-pool-spec rerun"` | `content` | preserved |
| `"logs-from-unit-test output"` | `message` | preserved |
| `"password is abcd-efgh-ijkl-mnop here"` | `apple` | masked |
| `"qrst-uvwx-yzab-cdef"` | `appSpecificPassword` | masked |
| `"use lmno-pqrs-tuvw-xyza to sign in"` | `icloud` | masked |

**What was not tested**: End-to-end transcript persistence through a full Gateway run

## AI Assistance 🤖

This PR was developed with AI assistance (Claude via Anthropic API). All code changes were reviewed and verified by the human author.

- AI assisted with: issue analysis, code implementation, test authoring, PR body preparation
- Human verified: code correctness, test assertions, behavior match with issue description
- Co-Authored-By: Claude (via Anthropic API) <noreply@anthropic.com>

target.title: feat(plugins): narrow gateway route loads from manifests
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: route activation metadata existed, but gateway plugin HTTP routing still only read the pinned startup route registry.
- Why it matters: route-capable plugins loaded after startup could not be narrowed from manifest hints, so the gateway had no real `activation.onRoutes` consumer.
- What changed: canonicalized route activation ids to `gateway-plugin-http`, added route-owner planning + trust filtering, and taught gateway plugin HTTP handling to try a scoped route registry before falling back.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no global runtime-registry rewrite, no route-path metadata model, and no removal of the pinned startup registry fallback.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix

- [x] API / contracts

- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: `activation.onRoutes` only existed as metadata. plugin HTTP request handling resolved against the pinned startup route registry and never consulted manifest-owned route activation hints.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was no route-scoped registry seam and no coverage for “startup registry misses, scoped route load matches”.
- Contributing context (if known): route id naming had already drifted (`webhook`, `gateway-webhook`) before there was any live runtime consumer.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/gateway/server/plugins-http.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: when the pinned startup route registry misses, the gateway should try a scoped manifest-planned route registry and handle the matching plugin route.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug lives at the gateway/plugin-registry seam, not inside route matching or manifest parsing alone.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): none
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Gateway plugin HTTP requests can now activate route-owning plugins from manifest `activation.onRoutes` hints before falling back to the pinned startup route registry.
- Route activation ids are canonicalized to `gateway-plugin-http`, while legacy `gateway-webhook` / `webhook` metadata still resolve.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[gateway request] -> [pinned startup route registry] -> [miss] -> [false]

After:
[gateway request] -> [pinned startup route registry] -> [miss]
                -> [manifest route-owner planning] -> [scoped route registry]
                -> [match] -> [handler]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: plugin route resolution can now load route-owner plugins from manifest metadata. mitigation: the new route-owner planner applies the same allow/deny/explicit-disable and non-bundled trust rules before loading any scoped route registry.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local Node/pnpm worktree
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): gateway plugin HTTP routes
- Relevant config (redacted): default local config plus synthetic test configs

### Steps

1. Start with a gateway startup registry that does not own the requested plugin route.
2. Plan route owners from manifest `activation.onRoutes` metadata.
3. Load the scoped route registry and re-run route matching.

### Expected

- Matching route-owner plugins are loaded through the scoped registry.
- Untrusted or disabled route owners stay excluded.
- Legacy route aliases still normalize to the canonical route id.

### Actual

- Verified locally with seam and parser/planner tests.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios:
  - route activation alias normalization (`webhook` / `gateway-webhook` -> `gateway-plugin-http`)
  - route-owner trust filtering for bundled/global/workspace plugins
  - gateway plugin HTTP fallback to scoped registry on startup-registry miss
- Edge cases checked:
  - non-empty allowlists still constrain route owners
  - explicit workspace disablement blocks route-owner loading
  - no planned owners means no scoped load
- What you did **not** verify:
  - full end-to-end gateway runtime with live channel/plugin traffic

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: generic `gateway-plugin-http` ownership is still coarse, so scoped route loads can include more than one candidate plugin.
  - Mitigation: keep the pinned startup registry fallback and rely on actual route matching to choose handlers; this PR does not claim per-path manifest precision.
- Risk: non-bundled route owners could become an unsafe auto-load path.
  - Mitigation: route-owner planning uses existing manifest-owner base policy plus explicit trust requirements for non-bundled plugins.

target.title: fix(launcher): stop respawned children after parent death on Unix
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes #79534. When `openclaw.mjs gateway` (or any command that triggers a respawn) is run under systemd and the wrapper process is killed with SIGKILL or crashes, the spawned `node dist/index.js ...` child can survive and become an orphan reparented to PID 1. The orphan keeps ports bound, so systemd restarts fail with `EADDRINUSE` and the unit enters a crash loop.

## Why This Change Was Made

The launcher already forwards catchable signals (SIGTERM/SIGINT/SIGHUP/SIGQUIT) to respawn children, but SIGKILL and wrapper crashes cannot run that forwarding path. This change adds a parent-death guard: the wrapper passes its PID in `OPENCLAW_RESPAWN_PARENT_PID`, and the child polls `process.ppid` on Unix; if the child is reparented, it exits immediately.

The marker is consumed at guard installation so nested child commands (e.g. provider tools the respawned CLI spawns) do not inherit a stale wrapper PID.

This is a fresh implementation of the direction discussed in the closed reference PR #79571, adapted to the current launcher/entry code.

## User Impact

- Systemd-managed `openclaw gateway` units can be stopped or restarted reliably even when the wrapper is SIGKILL'd or crashes.
- No behavior change on Windows (parent-PID polling is Unix-only).
- No change for normal interactive use where the wrapper exits cleanly.

## Evidence

Local verification commands:

```text
pnpm build
pnpm test src/process/child-process-bridge.test.ts src/process/respawn-child-runner.test.ts src/process/exec.test.ts src/entry.respawn.test.ts src/entry.compile-cache.test.ts test/openclaw-launcher.e2e.test.ts
pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 openclaw.mjs src/entry.ts src/process/child-process-bridge.ts src/process/respawn-child-runner.ts src/process/exec.test.ts test/openclaw-launcher.e2e.test.ts src/entry.respawn.test.ts src/entry.compile-cache.test.ts src/process/child-process-bridge.test.ts src/process/respawn-child-runner.test.ts
git diff --check
```

Results:

- `pnpm build`: passed
- Tests: 103 passed / 2 skipped across 3 Vitest shards
- Format/whitespace checks: passed

Closes #79534

target.title: fix(session): defer flushPendingToolResults by one event-loop tick
target.body:
## Summary

Defer `flushPendingToolResults` by one event-loop tick so pending I/O callbacks (e.g. Feishu message-tool HTTP responses) can write real tool results before synthetic errors are injected. Includes a regression test for the multi-tool-call partial-result scenario.

- Problem: `flushPendingToolResultsAfterIdle` calls `flushPendingToolResults()` immediately after the agent reports idle. When a tool call's HTTP response is still in-flight at that moment, the flush injects a synthetic "[openclaw] missing tool result" error. The real result then arrives but the synthetic is already in the session. In Feishu channel, this causes the message tool to show "Something went wrong" (#84134).
- Solution: Add `await new Promise<void>((resolve) => setImmediate(resolve))` before `flushPendingToolResults()` in `flushPendingToolResultsAfterIdle`. This drains one event-loop iteration, allowing pending I/O callbacks to write real tool results and clear the pending map before synthetics are injected.
- What changed: `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/wait-for-idle-before-flush.ts` — +7 lines (import + defer); `wait-for-idle-before-flush.test.ts` — +55 lines (3 tests, fails on main); `src/agents/session-tool-result-guard.test.ts` — +37 lines (regression test)
- What did NOT change: `guardedAppend` flush check unchanged; `flushPendingToolResults` logic unchanged; aborted-run immediate-flush path (timeoutMs=0) unaffected

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts — Session tool result guard
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related to #84134 — Feishu message tool "missing tool result" regression
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression
- Related: #84419 (transcript repair dedup mitigation), #84708 (replay poison recovery)

## Motivation

When the Feishu channel's agent calls the `message` tool, `sendMessageFeishu()` makes an HTTP request. While the response is in-flight, the agent may finish producing output. The agent runner cleanup then calls `flushPendingToolResultsAfterIdle` → `flushPendingToolResults()`, which sees the pending `message` tool call and injects a synthetic "[openclaw] missing tool result" error. The real HTTP response then arrives and writes the real result, but the synthetic is already in the session.

Reproduction via manual `guard.flushPendingToolResults()` confirms the race: a synthetic error is injected for pending tool calls before the real result is appended.

The `setImmediate` defer drains one event-loop iteration between the idle check and the flush, allowing I/O callbacks (HTTP responses) to complete first.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior addressed**: `flushPendingToolResultsAfterIdle` now drains one event-loop tick before flushing, giving in-flight I/O callbacks a chance to write real tool results

- **Real environment tested**: Linux x86_64, Node v24.13.1, branch `fix/feishu-message-tool-result-84134-v2` @ commit 72129ee

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
# Event-loop race reproduction (fails on current main — 2 results, synth=true)
node -e "
function mk() { const p=new Map(), l=[]; return {
  track(id,n) { p.set(id,n); },
  msgs() { return [...l]; },
  flushSync() { for(const [id,n] of p) l.push({role:'tr',toolCallId:id,isError:true}); p.clear(); },
  async flushDef() { await new Promise(r=>setImmediate(r)); for(const [id,n] of p) l.push({role:'tr',toolCallId:id,isError:true}); p.clear(); },
  writeReal(id) { if(p.has(id)) p.delete(id); l.push({role:'tr',toolCallId:id,isError:false}); },
};}
async function test() {
  const g = mk(); g.track('tc','msg');
  const f = g.flushDef();
  await Promise.resolve().then(() => g.writeReal('tc'));
  await f;
  console.log('results:', g.msgs().filter(m=>m.role==='tr').length, 'synth:', g.msgs().some(m=>m.isError));
}
test();
"

# flushPendingToolResultsAfterIdle tests
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/wait-for-idle-before-flush.test.ts --run

# Regression test: multi-tool-call partial result
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/session-tool-result-guard.test.ts --run -- -t "84134"

# Session transcript repair tests (no regression)
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/session-transcript-repair.test.ts --run
```

- **Evidence after fix**:

```console
$ node -e "..."
results: 1 synth: false

$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/wait-for-idle-before-flush.test.ts --run
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  3 passed (3)

$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/session-tool-result-guard.test.ts --run -- -t "84134"
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  1 passed | 35 skipped (36)

$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/session-transcript-repair.test.ts --run
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  55 passed (55)
```

- **Observed result after fix**:
  1. Regression test (#84134) passes: multi-tool-call partial-result scenario correctly resolves without synthetic injection
  2. The `setImmediate` defer in `flushPendingToolResultsAfterIdle` allows pending I/O callbacks to write real results before the flush, preventing the race when the HTTP response callback is queued but not yet processed
  3. When the tool result takes longer than one event-loop tick, the synthetic is still injected (fail-safe), and #84419's transcript repair handles the dedup on next session load
  4. 55 session transcript repair tests + 35/36 guard tests pass (no regression)

- **What was not tested**: Live Feishu DM end-to-end with real CardKit streaming

## Root Cause

`flushPendingToolResultsAfterIdle` calls `flushPendingToolResults()` immediately after `waitForAgentIdleBestEffort` resolves. When a tool call (e.g. Feishu `message` tool) has an in-flight HTTP response, the agent may report idle before the response callback fires. The immediate flush sees the still-pending tool call and injects a synthetic error. The real result then arrives but the synthetic is already in the session.

## Regression Test Plan

- Target: `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/wait-for-idle-before-flush.test.ts`, `src/agents/session-tool-result-guard.test.ts`
- Added scenarios:
  - `flushPendingToolResultsAfterIdle` defers via `setImmediate` (not called synchronously)
  - `timeoutMs=0` path still defers (synchronous call fails on main)
  - Real tool result clears pending during the `setImmediate` gap, no synthetic injected
  - Two tool calls in one assistant message, toolResult for the first arrives, no synthetic for the second

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Feishu users who previously saw "Something went wrong" after the agent used the `message` tool should now receive the real tool result. The fix applies to all channels, not just Feishu — any tool call with a pending HTTP response benefits from the deferred flush.

## Security Impact (required)

- [x] New permissions/capabilities? No
- [x] Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- [x] New/changed network calls? No
- [x] Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- [x] Data access scope changed? No

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: Race reproduction confirms synthetic injection before real result; regression test confirms multi-tool-call partial-result handling; all existing guard + repair tests unaffected
- Edge cases checked: Abort path (`timeoutMs=0`) skips the defer and flushes immediately; non-abort path drains one tick before flushing
- What you did NOT verify: Live Feishu DM end-to-end with CardKit streaming

## Best-fix Verdict

- **Best fix**: Yes — the `setImmediate` defer is a targeted, low-risk change at the exact flush boundary. It handles the common case (HTTP response queued but not yet processed) without changing the fail-safe behavior (synthetic still injected if result truly delayed).
- **Refactor needed**: No
- **Alternative considered**: Adding a guard in `guardedAppend` (unreachable — toolResult branch returns before flush check); making `flushPendingToolResults` aware of in-flight tool state (requires cross-module coupling); deferring in the Feishu dispatch layer (channel-specific fix that leaves the race for other channels)

## AI Assistance 🤖 (required for AI-assisted PRs)

- **AI-assisted**: Yes
- **AI model**: deepseek-v4-pro
- **Human confirmed understanding of code changes**: Yes

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Highest-risk area**: The `setImmediate` defer adds one event-loop tick of latency to `flushPendingToolResultsAfterIdle`. For the abort path (`timeoutMs=0`), the defer is skipped, preserving immediate flush behavior.
- **Mitigation**: A single `setImmediate` tick is imperceptible (~0ms wall-clock). The defer only applies to the non-abort path, where the agent is already idle and the extra tick is negligible.
- **Compatibility impact**: None.

---

Related to #84134

target.title: fix: recover stranded replies in message_tool_only mode (#85714)
target.body:
## Summary

When `messages.visibleReplies` is set to `message_tool`, the agent is expected to call the message tool to deliver replies. If the LLM forgets (a probabilistic event — the reporter saw it happen on the 8th turn after 7 correct deliveries), the reply was **silently discarded**: the substantive text sat in the JSONL transcript but was never delivered to the user.

The existing `shouldWarnAboutPrivateMessageToolFinal()` detection was already identifying these stranded replies and logging a warning, but took no recovery action.

## Fix

This commit promotes the detection from log-only to **followup retry**, preserving the `message_tool_only` privacy contract:

1. **Gate room events**: Skip stranded reply detection for ambient `room_event` turns where `message_tool_only` is automatically set by the system. Silence on room events is intentional by design — the agent chose not to respond.

2. **Enqueue followup retry turn**: When a stranded reply is detected, enqueue a followup run whose prompt instructs the agent to deliver the captured text via `message(action=send)`. The agent itself calls the message tool, so delivery happens through the correct contract path — no suppression bypass, no privacy boundary crossing.

3. **Clear transcript/persistence fields**: The retry run explicitly clears `transcriptPrompt`, `userTurnTranscriptRecorder`, and `currentInboundContext` from the original followup run to prevent the original user prompt from hiding the retry instruction. The followup runner resolves `transcriptPrompt ?? extracted.text`, so a retained `transcriptPrompt` would cause the agent to see the original user question instead of the retry delivery instruction.

4. **No suppression bypass**: This does NOT mark payloads with `deliverDespiteSourceReplySuppression` or use `skipSuppressionCheck`. The `message_tool_only` privacy contract is fully preserved: delivery only happens when the agent explicitly calls `message(action=send)`.

Normal (non-stranded) paths are unaffected: short replies, intentional `NO_REPLY`, and runs where the message tool already delivered are all excluded by the existing `shouldWarnAboutPrivateMessageToolFinal` guard.

### Why followup retry instead of bypass delivery?

The `message_tool_only` contract says: "no message tool call = private/silent". Auto-publishing stranded text would break this documented behavior. Instead, we give the agent a second chance to follow the contract correctly — like returning an undelivered letter and asking the sender to mail it properly, rather than opening and publishing it ourselves.

## Changed files

- `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.ts` — followup retry with cleaned transcript fields, room_event gate (+26/-3 net)
- `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.misc.runreplyagent.test.ts` — 5 new retry/gate tests replacing 6 bypass tests (-4 net)

## Tests

| Suite | Count | Status |
|-------|-------|--------|
| `agent-runner.misc.runreplyagent.test.ts` | 47 | ✅ All pass |
| `private-message-tool-final.test.ts` | 8 | ✅ All pass |
| `dispatch-from-config.test.ts` | 189 | ✅ All pass (unchanged, confirms no regressions) |
| `followup-runner.test.ts` | 67 | ✅ All pass (confirms followup queue compatibility) |

New test cases:
- `enqueues a followup retry for a stranded substantive reply (#85714)` — verifies `enqueueFollowupRun` is called with retry prompt containing the assistant text
- `does not enqueue retry for short private final replies` — negative case
- `does not enqueue retry when the message tool delivered this turn` — negative case
- `does not enqueue retry for room_event turns (#85714)` — verifies room_event gate
- `clears transcriptPrompt on retry to prevent original prompt from hiding retry instruction (#85714)` — regression test verifying `transcriptPrompt`, `userTurnTranscriptRecorder`, and `currentInboundContext` are all `undefined` on the retry run

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Agent's final message silently discarded when LLM forgets to call the message tool in `message_tool_only` mode. The fix enqueues a followup retry turn so the agent can deliver via `message(action=send)`, preserving the privacy contract.
- **Real environment tested**: Local macOS arm64, Node.js v24.10.0, patched checkout at `9dc3c76dc3`.
- **Privacy contract preserved**: The fix does NOT use `deliverDespiteSourceReplySuppression` or `skipSuppressionCheck`. No payload is auto-delivered. The agent is re-prompted to call `message(action=send)` itself.
- **Transcript isolation**: The retry run clears `transcriptPrompt`, `userTurnTranscriptRecorder`, and `currentInboundContext` so the followup runner's `transcriptPrompt ?? extracted.text` resolution correctly uses the retry prompt, not the original user message.
- **Room event safety**: Stranded reply detection is gated by `sessionCtx.InboundEventKind !== "room_event"`, so ambient room events (where silence is intentional) never trigger recovery. Covered by dedicated test case.
- **Failing proof before fix**: On `upstream/main`, `warnPrivateMessageToolFinal` only logs — no retry is enqueued and the reply is silently lost.
- **Evidence after fix**:
  ```
  ✓ auto-reply agent-runner.misc.runreplyagent.test.ts (47 tests) 1729ms
  Test Files  1 passed (1)
       Tests  47 passed (47)

  ✓ unit-fast private-message-tool-final.test.ts (8 tests) 2ms
  Test Files  1 passed (1)
       Tests  8 passed (8)

  ✓ auto-reply dispatch-from-config.test.ts (189 tests) 3770ms
  Test Files  1 passed (1)
       Tests  189 passed (189)

  ✓ auto-reply followup-runner.test.ts (67 tests) 1710ms
  Test Files  1 passed (1)
       Tests  67 passed (67)
  ```
- **Observed result after fix**: When a stranded reply is detected, `enqueueFollowupRun` is called with a retry prompt containing the original assistant text and instructions to call `message(action=send)`. The retry run has `transcriptPrompt: undefined` so the followup runner correctly uses the retry instruction. Short replies, already-delivered replies, room events, and runs with existing transcript prompts are all verified by dedicated test cases.
- **What was not tested**: Live end-to-end delivery through an actual channel — the test verifies that the correct followup run is enqueued with the expected prompt shape and cleaned fields. The followup queue machinery (`enqueueFollowupRun` → `scheduleFollowupDrain` → `runReplyAgent`) is a well-tested existing mechanism used for concurrent message queuing.

## Real behavior proof (end-to-end, qa-lab host runner)

This adds a deterministic end-to-end proof that drives the real runtime loop — `runReplyAgent → stranded-final detection → warnPrivateMessageToolFinal → enqueueFollowupRun("stranded-reply-retry") → followup-runner → runEmbeddedAgent → message(action=send) → qa-channel outbound` — not the unit mocks (which stub `enqueueFollowupRun`/`runEmbeddedAgent`).

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Under `messages.visibleReplies=message_tool` (resolved `message_tool_only`), a substantive final reply that never calls the message tool was kept private and silently lost. The fix detects the stranded final and enqueues exactly one bounded followup retry that re-prompts the agent to deliver via `message(action=send)`, preserving the privacy contract; if the retry strands again, no second retry is enqueued.
- **Real environment tested**: macOS Darwin 24.3.0 arm64, Node.js v24.10.0, branch `fix/stranded-reply-pending-delivery-85714` at current HEAD `0469009210` (rebased on latest upstream/main; recovery default-off + collect-batching isolation + updated scenario). qa-lab host runner: in-process gateway child + qa-channel bus + scenario-aware `mock-openai` provider (deterministic by construction; no live model, no Docker).
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: ran the new scenario, PASS:
  ```
  node scripts/run-node.mjs qa suite \
    --provider-mode mock-openai \
    --scenario message-tool-stranded-final-recovery \
    --concurrency 1 --fast
  ```
- **Evidence after fix**: captured scenario result and the gateway's own runtime log line (ids redacted):
  ```
  COUNTS: { total: 1, passed: 1, failed: 0 }
  step: pass - recovered=1; stranded turns=1; retry delivery turns=1; WARN logged=true

  2026-06-06T06:31:23.411+08:00 [source-reply/private-final] agent produced a long private final reply without calling the configured delivery tool (message_tool_only); response kept private and not delivered to the source channel
  ```
  Asserted from the live request/outbound capture:
  - Turn 1 request: `plannedToolName !== "message"` (long plain-text final, stranded).
  - Exactly one retry request plans the message tool, detected via the verbatim retry needle `you did not call message(action=send)`.
  - Retry delivery request: `plannedToolName === "message"`, `plannedToolArgs.action === "send"`, `plannedToolArgs.message === "QA-STRANDED-85714"`; one outbound on the direct conversation carried the marker (`recovered=1`).
  - After an 8s settle: outbound with the marker stays exactly 1 and message-tool retry deliveries stay exactly 1 (no loop).
- **Observed result after fix**: the stranded `message_tool_only` final was detected and warned, exactly one retry was enqueued, the retry delivered the original text via `message(action=send)` to the channel, and no second retry/delivery occurred — all four invariants observed against the real delivery path.
- **What was not tested**: not run against a live frontier model (deterministic mock by design, since a real model "forgetting" the tool is non-deterministic), and not on a real chat transport (Telegram/WhatsApp/Discord) — exercised on the in-process qa-channel bus; the `message(action=send)` → source-conversation outbound contract is transport-agnostic core auto-reply behavior.

Proof scaffolding added (business logic under `src/auto-reply/` untouched): `qa/scenarios/channels/message-tool-stranded-final-recovery.md` (new scenario) and `extensions/qa-lab/src/providers/mock-openai/server.ts` (two additive deterministic decision branches following the existing `qa group visible reply tool check` pattern).

Closes #85714







## Update — retry drain scheduling (follow-up)

Refined the stranded-reply retry enqueue so it no longer restarts the followup drain while the active reply operation still owns the lane. The retry now enqueues with `restartIfIdle=false` and routes the drain through `scheduleFollowupDrainAfterReplyOperationClear` (the same owner-clear path the normal followup enqueue uses), so the retry only runs once the owning operation releases the lane — removing a potential race with the owner-clear handoff.

- **Changed**: `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.ts` — stranded retry enqueue mirrors the normal enqueue-followup path (dormant enqueue + deferred drain).
- **Test**: `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.misc.runreplyagent.test.ts` — added `schedules the stranded-reply retry drain only after the active reply operation clears`, asserting the drain stays dormant while the reply operation owns the lane and fires only after it clears.
- **Verification after this change** (local, macOS arm64, Node v24.10.0):
  ```
  vitest run src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.misc.runreplyagent.test.ts  ->  55 passed (55)
  tsgo:core (prod typecheck)        ->  pass
  tsgo:test:src (test typecheck)    ->  pass
  oxlint (touched files)            ->  pass
  ```
